summary: High‑energy spring ’71 set with blazing Good Lovin' jam and tight classics
blurb: The show delivers scorching guitar work, a marathon Good Lovin' jam, and punchy renditions of Sugar Magnolia and Hard To Handle. Jerry’s tone is described as glistening and on‑fire while Phil lays down buttery bass, making this one of the tighter spring ’71 performances.
review: On April 13, 1971 at Scranton's Catholic Youth Center, the Dead turned in a ferocious two‑set marathon that still feels fresh. From an opening Casey Jones that set a frantic pace to a second‑set climax featuring a 20‑minute Good Lovin' stretch, the band was locked in. Jerry Garcia’s guitar shone – glistening, shredding and even slipping into pedal‑steel textures with an iconic wah solo on Sugar Magnolia – while Phil Lesh provided that “sitting in your lap” bass foundation that kept everything grounded. Bob Weir added his vocal grit on classics like Casey Jones and Good Lovin', and Pigpen’s harmonica erupted on Big Boss Man, giving the set a raw blues edge. The rhythm section, especially Bill Kreutzmann’s drums, drove the high‑energy flow with power and precision. Across all four soundboard sources the performance is consistently praised as tight, vibrant, and one of the standout shows from the spring ’71 tour.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Three recordings are overwhelmingly positive and one is mixed, with no negative consensus. Reviewers repeatedly highlight stellar guitar work, a marathon Good Lovin' jam, tight rhythm section, and strong vocal performances – indicating strong positive sentiment across the show.

best_recording.reason: Remastered soundboard with clear depth, excellent guitar capture and overall very listenable despite minor clipping.

key_highlights: 20‑minute Good Lovin' jam that builds into a soaring climax
Pedal‑steel textures and iconic wah solo on Sugar Magnolia
Pigpen’s fiery harmonica on Big Boss Man and driving drums from Bill Kreutzmann
Tight transitions and high‑energy flow throughout both sets

band_performance.Jerry: Guitar is glistening, shredding and off‑the‑charts throughout, with pedal‑steel textures and an iconic wah solo on Sugar Magnolia.
band_performance.Phil: Bass sits in your lap, delivering a warm, buttery foundation and tight interplay that drives the high‑energy set.
band_performance.Bob: Vocal presence shines on Casey Jones, Good Lovin' and other numbers, blending harmonies with Jerry’s lead.
band_performance.Ron: Pigpen’s organ is faint in the mix but his harmonica erupts on Big Boss Man, adding a hot, inspirational edge.
band_performance.Billy: Bill Kreutzmann’s drumming is praised for its power and precision, especially during the extended Good Lovin' jam.
